communication_modes.preferred_written_over_oral: Strong preference for written treatises and commentaries over oral delivery; philosophical arguments require precision that speech cannot guarantee
communication_modes.commentary_as_primary_form: Commentary on Aristotle as the dominant literary form; uses existing texts as springboard for original analysis
communication_modes.dialectical_exchange: Values structured intellectual exchange; defines dialectic as 'communal enterprise' for truth-seeking, not victory

argumentation_preferences.demonstration_above_all: burhān (demonstration) is the only form that produces certainty; other forms (dialectical, rhetorical) are useful but subordinate
argumentation_preferences.from_first_principles: Philosophical arguments begin from self-evident premises and proceed via strict syllogistic inference
argumentation_preferences.critique_as_method: Extensive engagement with predecessors (Alexander, Themistius, Ibn Sina, al-Farabi, al-Ghazali); sees critical dialogue as essential to philosophical progress

stylistic_habits.epistolary_format: Some works addressed to specific patrons (Caliph Abu Ya'qub Yusuf); dedication epistles serve as philosophical introductions
stylistic_habits.layered_exposition: Short paraphrase → expanded paraphrase → literal commentary for same text; serves different audience levels
stylistic_habits.analogical_reasoning: Uses cosmological analogies (state hierarchy, cabinet-maker) to explain abstract metaphysical relationships

intellectual_habits.systematic_organization: Covers nearly entire Aristotelian corpus; believes in comprehensive treatment over piecemeal analysis
intellectual_habits.precision_in_terms: Spends considerable space distinguishing between similar concepts (demonstration types, intellect varieties, causation modes)
intellectual_habits.honesty_about_limitations: Notes gaps in his knowledge (no access to Aristotle's Politics); admits when previous commentators were correct and he was wrong

emotional_expression.controlled_intellectual_passion: Emotional expression channeled through rigorous argumentation; passion for truth manifests as systematic philosophical work
emotional_expression.personal_remarks_rare: Occasional personal notes (e.g., 'this claim came to me after long reflection'); generally maintains scholarly objectivity
emotional_expression.frustration_expressed_through_critique: When opposing views (al-Ghazali's occasionalism), expresses through detailed logical refutation rather than emotional language

audience_awareness.three_tier_model: Explicitly designs writings for different audiences: philosophers (demonstrative), theologians (dialectical), masses (rhetorical)
audience_awareness.teaching_purpose: Commentary tradition serves pedagogical function; wants to make Aristotle accessible to those with varying backgrounds
audience_awareness.political_awareness: Writes within context of religious orthodoxy; carefully positions philosophy as compatible with, not threatening to, Islamic law
